title: Prim's MST (grow tree + min-heap)
code: static int PrimMst(int n, List<(int to, int w)>[] adj)
while (pq.TryDequeue(out int u, out int w))

frames.caption: Seed the min-PQ with the start vertex (0, weight 0). The tree is empty; the PQ holds candidate crossing edges.
frames.note: A minimum spanning tree connects all vertices with least total weight. Prim grows it from any start vertex, always absorbing the vertex reachable by the cheapest edge leaving the current tree. The min-heap makes "cheapest crossing edge" an O(log V) lookup.

frames.caption: Pop (0@0): add start vertex 0. total=0.
frames.note: Prim grows a single tree outward. The min-PQ always hands back the lightest edge leaving the current tree, so the vertex it attaches is joined by the cheapest possible link — that is the cut property applied locally to the cut (tree | rest). Unlike Dijkstra, the key is the single edge weight w, NOT a distance from a source: Prim minimizes the tree's total weight, not path length.

frames.caption: Push crossing edge 0-1 (w=1) onto the PQ.
frames.note: Relax from the new tree vertex: every edge to a vertex NOT yet in the tree is a candidate crossing edge, so push (neighbor, weight) onto the min-PQ. We may push several entries for the same vertex at different weights; the heap surfaces the smallest, and the larger duplicates get skipped later as stale. Edges to vertices already in the tree are ignored — they cannot grow it.

frames.caption: Push crossing edge 0-2 (w=3) onto the PQ.
frames.note: Relax from the new tree vertex: every edge to a vertex NOT yet in the tree is a candidate crossing edge, so push (neighbor, weight) onto the min-PQ. We may push several entries for the same vertex at different weights; the heap surfaces the smallest, and the larger duplicates get skipped later as stale. Edges to vertices already in the tree are ignored — they cannot grow it.

frames.caption: Pop the lightest crossing edge → add 1 via 0-1 (w=1). total=1.
frames.note: Prim grows a single tree outward. The min-PQ always hands back the lightest edge leaving the current tree, so the vertex it attaches is joined by the cheapest possible link — that is the cut property applied locally to the cut (tree | rest). Unlike Dijkstra, the key is the single edge weight w, NOT a distance from a source: Prim minimizes the tree's total weight, not path length.

frames.caption: Push crossing edge 1-2 (w=2) onto the PQ.
frames.note: Relax from the new tree vertex: every edge to a vertex NOT yet in the tree is a candidate crossing edge, so push (neighbor, weight) onto the min-PQ. We may push several entries for the same vertex at different weights; the heap surfaces the smallest, and the larger duplicates get skipped later as stale. Edges to vertices already in the tree are ignored — they cannot grow it.

frames.caption: Push crossing edge 1-3 (w=4) onto the PQ.
frames.note: Relax from the new tree vertex: every edge to a vertex NOT yet in the tree is a candidate crossing edge, so push (neighbor, weight) onto the min-PQ. We may push several entries for the same vertex at different weights; the heap surfaces the smallest, and the larger duplicates get skipped later as stale. Edges to vertices already in the tree are ignored — they cannot grow it.

frames.caption: Pop the lightest crossing edge → add 2 via 1-2 (w=2). total=3.
frames.note: Prim grows a single tree outward. The min-PQ always hands back the lightest edge leaving the current tree, so the vertex it attaches is joined by the cheapest possible link — that is the cut property applied locally to the cut (tree | rest). Unlike Dijkstra, the key is the single edge weight w, NOT a distance from a source: Prim minimizes the tree's total weight, not path length.

frames.caption: Push crossing edge 2-4 (w=5) onto the PQ.
frames.note: Relax from the new tree vertex: every edge to a vertex NOT yet in the tree is a candidate crossing edge, so push (neighbor, weight) onto the min-PQ. We may push several entries for the same vertex at different weights; the heap surfaces the smallest, and the larger duplicates get skipped later as stale. Edges to vertices already in the tree are ignored — they cannot grow it.

frames.caption: Pop (2@3) — but 2 is already in the tree: STALE, skip.
frames.note: This pop targets a vertex already in the tree: a lighter edge pulled it in earlier while this heavier duplicate sat in the heap. We skip it in O(1). Pushing duplicates and skipping stale pops is how Prim runs on a binary heap with no decrease-key — exactly the Dijkstra trick.

frames.caption: Pop the lightest crossing edge → add 3 via 1-3 (w=4). total=7.
frames.note: Prim grows a single tree outward. The min-PQ always hands back the lightest edge leaving the current tree, so the vertex it attaches is joined by the cheapest possible link — that is the cut property applied locally to the cut (tree | rest). Unlike Dijkstra, the key is the single edge weight w, NOT a distance from a source: Prim minimizes the tree's total weight, not path length.

frames.caption: Push crossing edge 3-4 (w=6) onto the PQ.
frames.note: Relax from the new tree vertex: every edge to a vertex NOT yet in the tree is a candidate crossing edge, so push (neighbor, weight) onto the min-PQ. We may push several entries for the same vertex at different weights; the heap surfaces the smallest, and the larger duplicates get skipped later as stale. Edges to vertices already in the tree are ignored — they cannot grow it.

frames.caption: Pop the lightest crossing edge → add 4 via 2-4 (w=5). total=12.
frames.note: Prim grows a single tree outward. The min-PQ always hands back the lightest edge leaving the current tree, so the vertex it attaches is joined by the cheapest possible link — that is the cut property applied locally to the cut (tree | rest). Unlike Dijkstra, the key is the single edge weight w, NOT a distance from a source: Prim minimizes the tree's total weight, not path length.

frames.caption: Pop (4@6) — but 4 is already in the tree: STALE, skip.
frames.note: This pop targets a vertex already in the tree: a lighter edge pulled it in earlier while this heavier duplicate sat in the heap. We skip it in O(1). Pushing duplicates and skipping stale pops is how Prim runs on a binary heap with no decrease-key — exactly the Dijkstra trick.

frames.caption: PQ drained, all 5 vertices in the tree → minimum spanning tree of weight 12.
frames.note: All 5 vertices are in the tree, joined by V-1 = 4 edges with no cycle: a spanning tree, and because every vertex entered by its cheapest crossing edge, the MINIMUM spanning tree. Weight 12 — the same tree Kruskal finds. With a binary heap Prim runs in O(E log V) time, a touch faster than Kruskal on dense graphs and structurally identical to Dijkstra.
